title: Amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner – Personal income tax - Canada.ca
content:
## Amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner – Personal income tax
- Provincial or territorial: Line 58640
- Tax year: 2025 | Prior tax years: 2024 and earlier
You may be able to claim all or part of certain unused non-refundable tax credits that your spouse or common-law partner qualifies for, if they do not need those amounts to reduce their own federal tax payable to zero.
If you are claiming amounts transferred for a spouse or common-law partner who died during the year, special rules may apply. To learn more: Line 32600 – Amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner, for someone who died .
- Who can claim the amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner
- How much you can claim

## Who can claim the amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner
A text version is available if needed.
Choose the situation that applies to you and scroll down for further questions and results.
Based on your selections above:
You cannot claim amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner because your spouse or common-law partner required the total amount that they qualified for to reduce their federal taxable income to zero.
Based on your selections above:
You can claim certain amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner on line 32600 of your tax return.
To calculate your claim, go to How much you can claim .
Based on your selections above:
You cannot claim amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner on your tax return because your spouse or common-law partner did not qualify for one or more non-refundable tax credit amounts.
Based on your selections above:
You cannot claim amounts transferred from a spouse or common-law partner on your tax return because you did not have a spouse or common-law partner in the tax year.
Based on your selections above:
You can claim the pension income amount. See line 31400 to find out how to calculate and report the pension income amount.
Based on your selections above:
You are not eligible to claim the pension income amount because the income that you reported on line 11500 , line 11600 or line 12900 was not listed in the chart or was not received as a result of the condition listed for that type of income.
Use this version if you do not want to answer the interactive questions .
Generally, you can claim the amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner if you meet all of the following conditions:
- You had a spouse or common‑law partner at the end of the year
You had a spouse or common‑law partner at the end of the year
- Your spouse or common-law partner qualify for one or more of the following non-refundable tax credit amounts:
Your spouse or common-law partner qualify for one or more of the following non-refundable tax credit amounts:
- the age amount ( line 30100 ) the Canada caregiver amount for infirm children under 18 years of age ( line 30500 ) the pension income amount ( line 31400 ) the disability amount for self ( line 31600 ) tuition, education and textbook amounts ( line 32300 )
- the age amount ( line 30100 )
- the Canada caregiver amount for infirm children under 18 years of age ( line 30500 )
- the pension income amount ( line 31400 )
- the disability amount for self ( line 31600 )
- tuition, education and textbook amounts ( line 32300 )
- Your spouse or common‑law partner did not need to use the total amount of the credits listed above that they qualified for to reduce their federal tax payable to zero.
Your spouse or common‑law partner did not need to use the total amount of the credits listed above that they qualified for to reduce their federal tax payable to zero.

### When you cannot claim transferred amounts
You cannot claim transferred amounts from your spouse or common-law partner in either of the following situations:
- You were separated because of a breakdown in your relationship for a period of 90 days or more that included December 31, 2025 .
- The amount is a tuition amount carried forward from a previous year (only current-year tuition may be transferred).

## How much you can claim
The amount you can claim is the unused portion of eligible non-refundable tax credits that your spouse or common-law partner did not need to reduce their own federal tax payable to zero.

### Calculate the amount you can claim
- Federal amount for 2025 To calculate your federal amount, complete the version of Schedule 2 below that applies to you: Schedule 2, Federal Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner (for all except QC and non-residents) Schedule 2, Federal Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner (for QC and non-residents only)

### Federal amount for 2025
To calculate your federal amount, complete the version of Schedule 2 below that applies to you:
- Schedule 2, Federal Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner (for all except QC and non-residents)
- Schedule 2, Federal Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner (for QC and non-residents only)
- Provincial and territorial amounts for 2025 The amount you can claim may vary depending on the province or territory you lived in at the end of the year : Alberta Complete Schedule AB(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. British Columbia Complete Schedule BC(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Manitoba Complete Schedule MB(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim . New Brunswick Complete Schedule NB(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Newfoundland and Labrador Complete Schedule NL(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Northwest Territories Complete Schedule NT(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Nova Scotia Complete Schedule NS(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Nunavut Complete Schedule NU(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim . Ontario Complete Schedule ON(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim . Prince Edward Island Complete Schedule PE(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Quebec Refer to: Revenu Québec Saskatchewan Complete Schedule SK(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim. Yukon Complete Schedule YT(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.

### Provincial and territorial amounts for 2025
The amount you can claim may vary depending on the province or territory you lived in at the end of the year :
Complete Schedule AB(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule BC(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule MB(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim .
Complete Schedule NB(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule NL(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule NT(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule NS(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule NU(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim .
Complete Schedule ON(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim .
Complete Schedule PE(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Refer to: Revenu Québec
Complete Schedule SK(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Complete Schedule YT(S2), Provincial Amounts Transferred from your Spouse or Common-Law Partner , to calculate your claim.
Claim the amounts transferred from your spouse or common-law partner on your federal tax return and your provincial or territorial Form 428 , if applicable.
- Federal tax return Enter the amount from line 13 of Schedule 2 on line 32600 of your federal tax return.
Enter the amount from line 13 of Schedule 2 on line 32600 of your federal tax return.
- Provincial or territorial Form 428 If you lived in a province or territory other than Quebec at the end of the year, enter the amount from your provincial or territorial Schedule 2 on line 58640 of your provincial or territorial Form 428 .

### Provincial or territorial Form 428
If you lived in a province or territory other than Quebec at the end of the year, enter the amount from your provincial or territorial Schedule 2 on line 58640 of your provincial or territorial Form 428 .

### Supporting documents
Do not send any supporting documents when you file your tax return. Keep them in case the CRA asks to see them later.
Attach your completed Schedule 2 to your paper return.
If your spouse or common-law partner is not filing a return, attach information slips showing their income.
Do not send other documents, but keep them in case the CRA asks to see them later.

### Give feedback about this page
Thank you for your feedback.
